refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts command latency, feedback fidelity, and operator situational awareness—all safety-critical factors in remote heavy equipment control. Refinement clarifies that the requirement is conditional on 'agreed network conditions' and ties acceptance to a measurable, customer-negotiated target rather than a fixed value.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth constraints) agreed with customer. Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle command execution → telemetry return). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit over a statistically significant sample (e.g., 10,000+ packets per test scenario).
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on command execution.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The network link shall be provisioned and monitored to maintain packet loss within agreed limits under specified worksite conditions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission protocols tuned to network conditions, with real-time packet loss monitoring and operator alerting.
DP-C: Provision dedicated, high-reliability network infrastructure (e.g., private LTE, bonded connections) with QoS guarantees to meet packet loss targets.
DP-D: Combine DP-B (adaptive FEC/retransmission) with DP-C (dedicated network provisioning) and add DP-A (redundancy) as a defense-in-depth strategy.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a multi-layered approach combining (1) dedicated, provisioned network infrastructure with QoS guarantees, (2) adaptive forward error correction and selective retransmission protocols with real-time monitoring, and (3) redundant communication channels with automatic failover. This approach addresses packet loss at the network layer (infrastructure), the protocol layer (FEC/retransmission), and the system layer (redundancy), providing resilience across multiple failure modes.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation system design (FEC/retransmission logic) with network infrastructure provisioning (dedicated link, QoS). This is acceptable because both are necessary to meet the packet loss requirement; however, it introduces a dependency on network provisioning decisions that may be outside the NGHE solution's direct control.

Redundant channels (DP-A component) couple with operator interface design: the operator must be aware of failover events and any transient loss of telemetry during switchover. This requires coordination with the Safety Services module.
Adaptive FEC/retransmission (DP-B component) couples with telemetry and tasking interface definitions: protocol overhead and latency trade-offs must be negotiated with the Teleoperation module.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is moderate and manageable. The three sub-strategies (infrastructure, protocol, redundancy) are largely orthogonal and can be developed in parallel. The primary coupling risk is between network provisioning (external to NGHE) and the teleoperation system's ability to exploit QoS guarantees.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer will provision or approve dedicated network infrastructure with QoS guarantees (e.g., private LTE, bonded broadband). If not, DP-D's effectiveness is severely limited.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite network conditions are stable and predictable enough to tune FEC/retransmission parameters. Highly variable conditions may require dynamic parameter adjustment.
RISK: Redundant channels increase system complexity, cost, and power consumption. Operator training and failover transparency are critical.
RISK: FEC adds latency and bandwidth overhead. If network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may worsen overall performance.
RISK: Automatic failover may introduce transient loss of telemetry, creating a safety hazard if not properly managed (e.g., operator must be alerted immediately).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Network provisioning strategy (owned by customer/site manager, outside NGHE scope), (2) Teleoperation protocol design (owned by Teleoperation module, includes FEC/retransmission), (3) Redundancy and failover logic (owned by Teleoperation or Safety Services, depending on safety criticality). Add a requirement that the NGHE solution shall provide configuration parameters and monitoring hooks to allow network operators to tune QoS settings. This clarifies ownership boundaries and reduces coupling.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication system, including protocol design, FEC/retransmission logic, redundant channel management, failover control, and real-time packet loss monitoring. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (to send commands and receive telemetry), the Safety Services module (to report communication health and receive safety constraints), and the Network Link (to transmit/receive data). The module is responsible for ensuring that packet loss remains within acceptable limits under agreed network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator commands (movement, tool control, etc.) flow from the operator console into the Teleoperation module via the Tasking interface. The module encodes these commands with FEC, selects redundant channels, and transmits to the Vehicle.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle state (position, sensor readings, equipment status) flows from the Vehicle module to the Teleoperation module via the Telemetry interface. The module decodes telemetry (correcting FEC errors if needed), monitors packet loss, and forwards to the operator console.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: The Teleoperation module receives safety constraints and alerts from the Safety Services module (e.g., 'do not execute commands if packet loss exceeds threshold'). It also sends communication health status to Safety Services (packet loss rate, failover events, channel status) to inform safety decisions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: The Teleoperation module transmits encoded commands and receives telemetry over the Network Link. It manages redundant channels, monitors link quality, and triggers failover if primary link degrades.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of teleoperation communication quality. All FEC, retransmission, redundancy, and failover logic should be contained within this module. Minimal diffusion: only the SafetyAlert interface requires coordination with Safety Services (acceptable, as safety constraints must be enforced).

Safety Services could own packet loss monitoring and alerting, but this would diffuse the teleoperation protocol logic across two modules. Not recommended as primary owner. However, Safety Services should own the safety policy (e.g., 'halt equipment if packet loss exceeds X%'), which it enforces via SafetyAlert.

Vehicle module owns the vehicle hardware and local control logic, not the remote communication system. Assigning packet loss responsibility to Vehicle would create high diffusion and poor separation of concerns.

NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. While it could own packet loss as a system-level requirement, delegating to the Teleoperation module (a child of NGHE) provides better modularity and clearer ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of DP-D (multi-layered packet loss mitigation). It contains the protocol stack, FEC/retransmission algorithms, redundant channel logic, and failover control. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimal. Safety constraints are enforced via SafetyAlert coordination with Safety Services, avoiding diffusion.
containment_risks: RISK: Network provisioning (dedicated infrastructure, QoS) is outside Teleoperation's direct control. The module must assume that the network layer provides the agreed-upon conditions. If network provisioning fails, DP-D cannot meet the packet loss requirement. Mitigation: Teleoperation should provide clear network requirements and monitoring hooks; Site Manager/Network Operator must validate provisioning.

RISK: Redundant channels may require hardware support (e.g., dual network interfaces on the vehicle). If Vehicle module does not provide this, Teleoperation cannot implement DP-A (redundancy). Mitigation: Clarify Vehicle module's network interface requirements early.

RISK: Failover logic may interact with Safety Services' command inhibition logic. If both modules attempt to halt equipment during a failover, race conditions or conflicting decisions could occur. Mitigation: Define clear ownership: Teleoperation detects failover and reports via SafetyAlert; Safety Services decides whether to inhibit commands.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the Teleoperation module's scope to explicitly exclude network infrastructure provisioning (that is the Site Manager's responsibility) but include configuration and monitoring interfaces that allow the Site Manager to verify that network conditions meet the agreed-upon targets. Add a sub-requirement: 'Teleoperation module shall provide a network diagnostics interface (e.g., real-time packet loss rate, latency, jitter) accessible to the Site Manager for validation and tuning.' This clarifies the boundary between Teleoperation's responsibilities (protocol, redundancy, monitoring) and the Site Manager's responsibilities (network provisioning, QoS configuration).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (multi-layered approach) directly addresses the refined FR (packet loss ≤ customer-defined threshold). The three sub-strategies (dedicated infrastructure, adaptive FEC/retransmission, redundancy) collectively provide multiple pathways to meet the requirement. The DP is neither over-constrained nor under-specified; it allows flexibility in implementation while ensuring the requirement is achievable. Coupling is moderate and manageable.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK (Low): FEC/retransmission logic is contained in Teleoperation. However, the Vehicle module must support the protocol (e.g., decode FEC-encoded commands). This is acceptable as a protocol dependency, not a diffusion of ownership.

DIFFUSION RISK (Low): Redundant channel failover may require coordination with Safety Services (e.g., Safety Services must inhibit commands during failover). This is managed via the SafetyAlert interface and is acceptable.

DIFFUSION RISK (Medium): Network provisioning and QoS configuration are outside Teleoperation's scope. If the Site Manager does not provision the network correctly, DP-D cannot succeed. Mitigation: Teleoperation must provide clear network requirements and diagnostics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation is the clear owner of DP-D. The module contains all protocol logic, redundancy control, and monitoring. External dependencies (network infrastructure, Safety Services constraints) are managed via well-defined interfaces. Containment score of 95 is justified.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible. Packet loss can be measured end-to-end under agreed network conditions. The Teleoperation module can provide diagnostics (real-time packet loss rate, FEC correction statistics, failover events) to support verification. Acceptance criteria (≤1% packet loss) are measurable and objective.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear. The Teleoperation module owns the teleoperation communication system and is responsible for meeting the packet loss requirement. The Site Manager owns network provisioning and must validate that agreed-upon network conditions are met. Safety Services owns the safety policy (e.g., command inhibition if packet loss exceeds threshold). Ownership boundaries are well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Add a sub-requirement to the refined FR: 'The NGHE solution shall provide real-time packet loss diagnostics and alerting to the Site Manager and Operator, enabling validation that packet loss remains within acceptable limits.' This supports verification and operational awareness.

Clarify the network provisioning responsibility in the project constraints or a separate Network Requirements document. Specify that the Site Manager is responsible for provisioning network infrastructure that meets the agreed-upon conditions (latency, jitter, bandwidth, packet loss baseline).

Define failover behavior explicitly: 'During a failover event (e.g., primary channel loss), the Teleoperation module shall alert the operator and Safety Services within [X] milliseconds. The operator shall retain control of the vehicle, but Safety Services may inhibit new commands until the failover is complete.' This clarifies the interaction between Teleoperation and Safety Services.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-defined and the selected DP (multi-layered approach) is sound. The Teleoperation module is the clear owner with high containment (95) and low diffusion (5). However, the FR is not atomic—it decomposes into four sub-FRs (detection, error correction, graceful degradation, network provisioning). To ensure complete coverage and avoid gaps, we must decompose to the next level and assign each sub-FR to appropriate modules. Additionally, the interaction between Teleoperation and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert) requires further refinement at the next level to define failover behavior and command inhibition logic.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time (ownership: Teleoperation).
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on command execution (ownership: Teleoperation).
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds (ownership: Teleoperation + Safety Services).

FR-NGHE-009.4: The network link shall be provisioned and monitored to maintain packet loss within agreed limits under specified worksite conditions (ownership: Site Manager / Network Operator, with monitoring support from Teleoperation).

assumptions: Customer will provision or approve dedicated network infrastructure with QoS guarantees (e.g., private LTE, bonded broadband).
Worksite network conditions are stable and predictable enough to tune FEC/retransmission parameters.
Vehicle module provides dual network interfaces (or equivalent) to support redundant channels.
Safety Services module can enforce command inhibition based on communication health status (SafetyAlert).
Operator is trained to recognize and respond to failover events and packet loss alerts.
open_risks: Network provisioning is outside NGHE's direct control. If the Site Manager does not provision the network correctly, the packet loss requirement cannot be met.
Redundant channels increase system complexity, cost, and power consumption. Trade-offs must be evaluated against safety and performance benefits.
FEC adds latency and bandwidth overhead. If network bandwidth is severely constrained, FEC may worsen overall performance.
Failover logic may interact with Safety Services' command inhibition logic, creating potential race conditions or conflicting decisions.
Operator situational awareness during failover events is critical. If the operator is not alerted immediately, they may issue commands that are delayed or lost.
evidence_needed: Network provisioning plan from Site Manager, specifying infrastructure, QoS settings, and baseline packet loss under agreed-upon conditions.
Vehicle module specification, confirming dual network interface support (or equivalent redundancy mechanism).
Teleoperation protocol specification, detailing FEC algorithm, retransmission logic, redundant channel management, and failover behavior.
Safety Services specification, defining command inhibition policy and SafetyAlert interface for communication health status.
Operator interface specification, showing how packet loss alerts and failover events are presented to the operator.
Test plan for packet loss measurement under representative network conditions, with acceptance criteria (≤1% packet loss).
